authoritative_sources.title: Incident Response Recommendations and Considerations for Cybersecurity Risk Management: A CSF 2.0 Community Profile
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST publishes federal cybersecurity standards and guidance; SP 800-61r3 is directly scoped to incident response recommendations and cybersecurity risk-management integration.

authoritative_sources.title: The NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST CSF 2.0 is a cross-industry cybersecurity risk-management framework that defines Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover outcomes.

authoritative_sources.title: Federal Government Cybersecurity Incident and Vulnerability Response Playbooks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA is the U.S. operational cyber agency; its playbooks provide incident and vulnerability response procedure language, coordination expectations, tracking, and remediation/recovery workflow references.

authoritative_sources.title: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 is a recognized federal control catalog with Incident Response controls and related audit, contingency, system integrity, and privacy controls.

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Critical Security Controls v8, Control 17 Incident Response Management
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS Controls are consensus-based safeguards used widely across sectors; Control 17 directly addresses incident response management, process, roles, communication, exercises, and post-incident reviews.

authoritative_sources.title: Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques into Incident Response
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-86 describes forensic evidence sources and forensic process integration with incident response while preserving legal and procedural caution, making it authoritative for evidence-boundary handling.

authoritative_sources.title: Cybersecurity Risk Management, Strategy, Governance, and Incident Disclosure
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The SEC final rule is authoritative for public-company cybersecurity incident disclosure obligations and therefore constrains incident communication and materiality language.

authoritative_sources.title: FTC Safeguards Rule: What Your Business Needs to Know
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FTC is the regulator responsible for Safeguards Rule enforcement; its business guidance describes written incident response plans, documentation, reporting, post-mortem, and governance reporting expectations for covered financial institutions.

authoritative_sources.title: Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure Act of 2022 (CIRCIA)
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA is the rulemaking and reporting authority for CIRCIA; its public guidance is authoritative for critical-infrastructure incident reporting posture and illustrates why reporting determinations require legal/regulatory routing.

workflow_stages.name: incident intake and authority check
workflow_stages.description: Confirm the user request, incident ticket, incident type, response stage, severity matrix, affected assets, owners, systems of record, tenant policy, and authority boundaries before producing analysis.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: evidence and telemetry assembly
workflow_stages.description: Map timeline, indicators, SIEM, EDR, identity, cloud, network, application, ticket, asset, backup, data classification, and evidence-store records to confirmed facts and missing gaps.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: severity, impact, and scope assessment
workflow_stages.description: Assess affected scope, business impact, data exposure indicators, response confidence, open assumptions, and control/safety implications without issuing legal materiality or breach conclusions.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: response option prioritization
workflow_stages.description: Prioritize containment, eradication support, recovery sequencing, monitoring, rollback, communication readiness, and hold or release prerequisites as recommendations to authorized owners.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: handoff and escalation
workflow_stages.description: Return to orchestrator or route to penetration testing, digital forensics, security operations, infrastructure, legal/regulatory, audit, crisis communications, architecture, resilience, or executive owners when authority shifts.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: post-incident review and corrective action
workflow_stages.description: Prepare debrief structure, recurrence signals, root-cause hypotheses, control gaps, corrective-action owners, and unresolved evidence questions without asserting final root cause where evidence is incomplete.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

artifacts: incident ticket, incident timeline, response stage, severity matrix, and incident commander or owner map
SIEM alerts, EDR detections, identity logs, cloud audit events, network telemetry, application logs, email security alerts, vulnerability context, and ticket notes
asset inventory, CMDB records, data classification, business service dependency map, backup and restore records, monitoring status, and containment state
tenant incident response policy, response playbooks, escalation tree, communication plan, legal/regulatory routing, evidence retention policy, and approval matrix

incident severity and impact assessment note, containment and recovery recommendation set, evidence gap checklist, escalation and communication brief, post-incident debrief, corrective-action tracker, and boundary refusal package
systems_of_record: identity provider and privileged access logs
cloud control-plane logs and cloud security posture records
network detection, firewall, proxy, DNS, email, and application log repositories
CMDB, asset inventory, service catalog, and owner directory
backup, restore, continuity, and monitoring systems
forensic evidence store and evidence-retention repository
communication archive, legal/regulatory tracker, audit evidence repository, and corrective-action tracker

allowed: classify incident response requests as in-scope, out-of-scope, or blocked on missing evidence, policy, systems, or authority
produce severity and impact assessment notes, evidence gap checklists, containment and recovery recommendation sets, communication readiness briefs, and post-incident control-gap hypotheses
prioritize recommendations by response stage, business impact, owner, confidence, prerequisite evidence, hold or release boundary, and rollback concern
route execution, forensic, legal, regulatory, audit, communications, architecture, resilience, and executive decisions to authorized owners
escalate: confidence is below threshold or critical telemetry, owner, severity, authority, or tenant policy records are missing
public guidance, tenant policy, contract terms, sector regulation, or jurisdiction-specific notification rules conflict
incident may involve regulated data, public-company disclosure, critical infrastructure, law enforcement, insurance, litigation, privilege, safety, or business-continuity authority
hold, release, recovery, service restoration, finding closure, or corrective-action approval depends on an authorized owner
refuse: execute vulnerability scans, exploitation, forensic tools, live remediation, patching, isolation, account changes, restore operations, or infrastructure changes
act as incident commander, crisis communications owner, legal representative, regulatory approver, audit attester, or executive decision maker
clear holds, release systems, close findings, assert final root cause, or certify recovery without documented evidence and owner approval
invent missing telemetry, infer unsupported scope or attribution, or treat conflicting records as resolved without documented adjudication

domain_failure_modes: generic cybersecurity guidance ignores incident-specific records such as incident tickets, telemetry, affected assets, owner maps, severity matrices, evidence stores, and response-stage state
the specialist overreaches into live containment, remediation, playbook execution, or incident command because the request uses incident response language
severity, impact, affected scope, data exposure, or root cause is asserted from incomplete SIEM, endpoint, identity, cloud, network, or asset evidence
a production hold, quarantine, recovery release, or corrective-action finding is cleared without documented approval and monitoring evidence
legal, SEC, FTC, CIRCIA, state breach, contractual, insurance, law-enforcement, or audit language is finalized without authorized review
forensic evidence is altered or chain-of-custody decisions are made by a non-forensic lane
tenant-specific thresholds, sector obligations, or stricter evidence-retention rules are ignored in favor of a generic public baseline
repeat incidents are treated as isolated events and corrective-action escalation is omitted

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Insufficient evidence to clear a hold or close a finding
Request to bypass a documented safety or quality control

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: jurisdiction-specific breach notification and sector reporting thresholds
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: Public sources differ by sector, jurisdiction, company status, and contract. The pack treats final notification timing, materiality, and language as legal/regulatory review work.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: CIRCIA final rule timing and covered-entity applicability
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: CISA guidance is tracked as active_watch. The pack flags critical-infrastructure reporting as tenant and legal/regulatory dependent instead of making a final determination.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: tenant-specific severity and recovery release criteria
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: Severity, hold, recovery, and release criteria must come from tenant policy, authority matrix, and service owner records at runtime.
